published.text:
### Hero
Badge: Use Case Guide
Heading: AI Appointment Setter for Service Businesses
Body: Stop losing leads to slow follow-up and after-hours silence. An AI appointment setter handles the repetitive parts of booking so your team focuses on the work that matters.
Sub: Below: what it actually does, which businesses benefit most, and how to decide if it is the right investment for yours.
CTA: Book a 30-Minute Call | All Guides

### What an AI appointment setter actually does
Intro: The term gets used loosely, so here is what it means in practice for a service business:
- Answers calls and messages: 24/7. Handles initial conversation, qualifying questions, basic service information.
- Books appointments directly: Checks calendar, offers slots, confirms bookings. Sends confirmations to both sides.
- Qualifies leads before booking: Asks screening questions. Routes qualified leads to booking, flags the rest.
- Sends reminders and reduces no-shows: Automated confirmation and reminder sequences. 20 to 40% no-show reduction typical.
- Follows up with leads who do not book: Automatic follow-up over next few days for leads who engage but do not complete booking.
- Hands off to your team when needed: Complex requests, complaints, or anything outside scope gets routed to a human with full context.

### Where it helps most
Six problem scenarios: Missed after-hours leads, Slow follow-up on inquiries, Staff time wasted on scheduling, High no-show rates, Inconsistent lead qualification, Seasonal volume spikes.

### Businesses that benefit most
Intro: AI appointment setters work best for businesses where revenue depends on booked appointments.
- Medical and dental practices: Patient scheduling, intake, reminders
- Home services (HVAC, plumbing, electrical): Service calls, estimates, dispatch
- Salons and spas: Booking, confirmations, waitlist management
- Legal and financial services: Consultation booking, qualification, follow-up
- Real estate: Showing scheduling, buyer/seller qualification
- Fitness and wellness: Class booking, personal training, trial sessions

### What an AI setter should not replace
Four boundaries: Complex consultations requiring empathy/negotiation, Complaint resolution, High-stakes decisions (medical/legal/financial advice), Relationship-building with key accounts.
Closing: The best results come from using AI for the repetitive 70 to 80% and freeing your team to focus on the interactions that actually require a person.

### Good fit / not a fit
Good fit: Revenue depends on appointments, losing after-hours leads, staff time on scheduling, clear booking flow, no-shows are costly.
Not a fit: Business does not rely on appointments, fewer than 10 inquiries/week, every interaction needs deep customization, no defined scheduling process, looking for a full sales closer.

### What this looks like in practice
Two case study cards (Restaurant voice agent, Instagram lead gen) linking to published pages.
Links to: /case-studies, /ai-automation-consultant-small-business

### FAQ — Common questions
5 Q&As covering: whether AI sounds robotic, what happens when AI cannot handle a question, setup timeline, cost, scheduling tool compatibility.

### Final CTA
Heading: Ready to stop losing leads to slow follow-up?
Body: Book a 30-minute call. We will look at your current booking process, identify where leads are falling through, and figure out the right setup for your business.
Sub: No hard sell. Just a clear look at whether an AI appointment setter makes sense for your situation.
Benefits: 30-minute focused call / Identify where leads are dropping off / Leave with a clear recommendation
Embedded booking widget.

edits.description: Use-case/decision page, not a blog post. Structured to help the reader evaluate fit, not just learn about the concept.
Hero leads with a problem-outcome statement, not a question.
"What it actually does" section demystifies the term with concrete, specific actions.
"What it should not replace" section builds credibility by being honest about limitations.
Tone is grounded and practical. Avoids "AI will transform your business" hype.
No em dashes in published copy. Uses periods, commas, or colons instead.
Cost and timeline numbers in FAQ are specific ranges, not vague. ROI claims are conservative ("most businesses recoup within the first month" not "guaranteed ROI").
Case study references link to real published pages.
Fit/non-fit section includes concrete disqualifiers (fewer than 10 inquiries/week is fine to handle manually).
CTA is problem-framed ("stop losing leads") not benefit-inflated.
Uses the site's standard Section wrapper, card patterns, and Framer Motion animations.
Booking widget is self-contained on this page.
